content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-3660               322-5901           322-6441                322-6444

November 19, 1982


Darrell Larsen
Sutter County Counsel
463 Second Street
Yuba City, CA  95991

                                        Re:  Telephone Advice Request for
                                             Michael Tomlinson
                                             Our No. A-82-192

Dear Mr. Larsen:

     Thank you for your telephone advice request of November 16,
1982.  Normally, we require more time in which to analyze the
facts of a particular request prior to rendering advice pursuant
to Government Code Section 83114.  However, the governmental
decision in question was to take place on the evening of the
16th; consequently, we rendered advice, at your request, on the
basis of somewhat sketchy facts.  Our understanding of those
facts is set forth below.  This letter will confirm and
substantiate our telephone advice to you of that date.

FACTS

     Pending before the Sutter County Planning Commission on
November 16, 1982, was a request for a rezone and general plan
amendment for a parcel of property to change from Agricultural
to Industrial.  The parcel is shown as Parcel "A" on the
enclosed diagram.  Parcel "A" is situated on the Southwest
corner of the intersection of two roads, one of which runs
North-South, the other of which runs East-West.  Parcel "A" is
owned by a brother "B" and sister "S" and a third party "P".

     Across the street, on the Northwest corner of the
intersection are two parcels, shown on the diagram as Parcel "X"
and Parcel "Y".  Parcel "X" is to the West and contiguous with
Parcel "Y".  "B" is the owner of Parcel "X", also zoned
Agricultural.  "P" is the owner of Parcel "Y", also zoned

Darrell Larsen
November 19, 1982
Page 2


Agricultural. "M" has placed "Y" up for sale and has listed "Y"
with Mr. Michael Tomlinson, a real estate broker. Mr. Tomlinson
is also the chair of the Sutter County Planning Commission.
Mr. Tomlinson has put together a sale of "Y". Originally, he
secured buyers who were four siblings (whom you have denoted as
the Farm Partnership, hereinafter "FP"). "FP" consists of four
sons of "B". As the deal began to close, it turned out that "B"
was also going to be involved in the purchase of "Y", in fact
"B" was going to finance the entire purchase for both himself
and "FP".

     At the time we last spoke (approximately 4:50 p.m. on
Nov. 16) it was your understanding that escrow had already been
opened and that it might well close prior to the meeting of the
Planning Commission that evening. Mr. Tomlinson will earn a
commission from that transaction and is the only broker
involved serving all parties to the transaction. The sale of
"Y" is not contingent upon the rezone and there is no present
intention on the part of the purchasers ("B" and "FP") to rezone
either "X" or "Y" from Agricultural to Industrial. They intend
to operate "Y" as a walnut orchard.

                              QUESTION

     You have asked advice on Mr. Tomlinson's behalf as to
whether or not he must disqualify himself from participation in
the rezone and general plan amendment decisions on Parcel "A".

                              ANALYSIS

     Mr. Tomlinson is a public official and the consideration by
the Planning Commission of the rezone and general plan amendment
for Parcel "A" is a governmental decision. Government Code
Section 87100. Therefore, Mr. Tomlinson may not participate in
any way in the decision (including chairing that portion of the
meeting) if he knows or has reason to know that he has a
financial interest in the decision. A "financial interest" in a
decision exists under Government Code Section 87103 whenever the
decision will have a material financial effect (distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally) on:

          (a)  Any business entity ....

          (b)  Any real property ....

. '

Darrell Larsen
November 19, 1982
Page 3

(C) Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided
to, received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is
made.

* * *

It is stipulated here that the decision on rezoning Parcel
"A" from Agricultural to Industrial will have a material
financial effect on the owners of Parcel "A", which effect will
be distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
(See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18702 and 18703, copies
enclosed.) The crux of the question you have asked is whether
the effect must fall directly upon Mr. Tomlinson and, if not,
whether any owner of Parcel "A" is a source of income to
Mr. Tomlinson.

The first part of your question may be answered simply. The
language of Government Code Section 87103 makes it clear that
the effect to be considered is on the business entity, the real
property, or the source of income. This is reinforced by the
Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. The
application of the law is confirmed by the court's opinion in
Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817; 139 Cal. Rptr. 161,
where the court states:

Morrow claims decisions concerning the
redevelopment in Linda Vista have no "material
financial effect" on him because he receives a set
amount each month. The unstated implication is even if
BEE were to reap large profits Morrow's salary would
remain the same. However, the statute is concerned
with whether there will be "a material financial effect
. . . on [a]ny source of income [over $250]. . . .
(Italics ours - Gov. Code, Section 87103.) . . .

* * *

Morrow asserts it is unconstitutional to
automatically disqualify a public official from
participating in decisions which may affect the
investments of an entity which pays him over $250 per
year. However, the whole purpose of the Political
Reform Act of 1974 is to preclude a government official
from participating in decisions where it appears he may
not be totally objective because the outcome will

Darrell Larsen
November 19, 1982
Page 4


     likely benefit a corporation or individual by whom he
is also employed.

* * *

     Morrow argues there is no conflict of interest
here because there is no evidence his activities as
president and attorney of BEE and his receipt of $550
per month would be affected in any way by any decision
on this project.  His concept of what constitutes a
conflict of interest is too narrow.  It is not just
actual improprieties which the law seeks to forestall
but also the appearance of possible improprieties.

* * *

                    Witt v. Morrow, Id at 822-23.

     We turn now to the question of whether any of the owners of
Parcel "A" is a source of income to Mr. Tomlinson.  "B" is an
owner of "A" and is also purchasing "Y" together with his four
sons ("FP"), but "B" is financing the entire transaction.
Mr. Tomlinson represented "B" in the transaction; no other
broker was involved.

     The Commission has long recognized that in real estate
transactions, as well as other sales situations, the purchaser
may be considered to be the source of the commission income
earned as well as the seller.  This is true even though on a
purely contractual basis the seller "pays" the commission. In
the Carey Opinion, 3 FPPC Opinions 99, No. 76-087, Nov. 3, 1977,
the Commission resolved the issue of the allocation of
commission income between the brokerage firm and the sales
agent.  However, in that discussion, the following language is
relevant to our consideration of the question at hand:

     Cornish and Carey [a real estate brokerage firm]
derives its income from commissions earned in
connection with real estate sales brokered by the
firm's salespersons.  Most of the firm's transactions
involve residential real esate, but it also has
salespersons who work with commercial and industrial
properties.
                    Carey Opinion, supra at 99.

* * *

Darrell Larsen
November 19, 1982
Page 5


     We conclude that the "fee" paid to the business
entity under the circumstances before us herein is only
that portion of the total commission retained by the
firm. We think that the proper characterization of the
transaction in question is that there are, in effect,
two separate payments subsumed in the one negotiable
instrument used by the buyer to pay the real estate
commission, one payment to the firm of Cornish and
Carey and another the salesperson who brokered the
sale.

     Although the firm of Cornish and Carey may be the
payee designated on the instrument used by the
purchaser to pay the commission, this is only done to
facilitate payment and avoid the necessity of having
the purchaser write two checks. The firm, in fact, has
no rights to the salesperson's portion of the
commission and, pursuant to the form contracts used by
the firm, is obligated to pay the salesperson his share
"immediately upon collecting or as soon thereafter as
practicable." . . . Thus, it would exalt form over
substance to conclude that the "fee" paid to the firm
is the entire commission.

     We note, moreover, that the payment to the
salesperson is inseparable from the transaction that
produces it. In other words, the payment is made with
funds derived from the particular sale that creates the
salesperson's right to the payment....

                    Id at 101-102.
                    (Emphasis added.)

     The situation involved here is directly analogous. The
buyer "B" writes one check, which goes into escrow. Out of that
check come disbursements to the seller and the broker, among
others. It is the sale which entitles Mr. Tomlinson to his
commission. In advice to Richard Platz, City Attorney of Blue
Lake on November 21, 1978, we specifically addressed the issue
of the source of real estate commission income. (M-78-136, copy
enclosed.)

     There we concluded that if the real estate salesperson
represented both the seller and buyer, then each was a source of
one-half of the commission income received. If only the seller
or the buyer was represented and another salesperson represented
the other party, then only the client of the salesperson in
question is the source of that salesperson's commission income.

Darrell Larsen
November 19, 1982
Page 6


     The analysis has been confirmed in several analogous
circumstances, most recently in our letter to D. B. Blain (No.
A-82-054, copy enclosed) where (at pp. 4-6) we discussed the
attribution of commission income for a travel agent, who
technically receives commission income from the provider rather
than the customer.

     Traditionally, commission income on the sale of agricultural
land such as Parcel "Y" is 5% or more of the sales price.
Consequently, 5% of the sales price is deemed received from "T"
and from "B" and "FP". It is presumed that "B's" pro rata share
of the commission income to Mr. Tomlinson will exceed $250.

     Lastly, we turn to the question of whether the commission
income from "B" has been "received by or promised to"
Mr. Tomlinson in the 12 months preceding the decision. Our
letter to Stanley Remeimeyer, A-81-510 (copy enclosed), answers
that question in the affirmative. There, we said that if the
sale had been consummated at the time of the decision, then the
income would be promised even if not actually received prior to
the decision. Consequently, where the sale is to the stage that
escrow has been opened and is on the verge of closing, the
commission income to Mr. Tomlinson is clearly in the "promised"
state, unless contingent upon the actions of a third party, such
as a lender. We were provided no evidence that such was the
case here, particularly given the very imminent closing of
escrow.

                              CONCLUSION

     I advised you that Mr. Tomlinson should disqualify himself
from participation in the rezone and general plan decisions
affecting Parcel "A" based upon the information which you were
able to furnish me at that time. This confirms that advice and
is in response to your specific request for substantiating
documentation.

     Should you have any further questions with regard to this
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me again. I may be
reached at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:plh
Enclosures

N

X                Y                A

82192

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked advice on Mr. Tomlinson's behalf as to 
whether or not he must disqualify himself from participating in 
the rezone and general plan amendment decisions on Parcel "A".

CONCLUSION: I advised you that Mr. Tomlinson should disqualify himself 
from participation in the rezone and general plan decisons 
affecting Parcel "A" based upon the information which you were 
able to furnish me at that time. 
This confirms that advice and 
is in response to your specific request for substantiating 
documentation.
